text:
Make lume installable on a repo that is not this one, as a Claude plugin, so a person who is not the author can run it and give feedback.

WHY NOW (strategic context). The first seven workstreams (build-lume -> seed-and-numbering) built a real, tested engine: deterministic, stdlib-only, schema-validated JSON state, an agent-friendly CLI with a self-describing verb catalog and --json everywhere, and a swappable TrackingStore. But every one of those workstreams improved lume-on-lume internals; not one narrowed the gap to the actual goal. Today lume v0.1.0 is usable by exactly one person, in exactly one repo, where .lume/ already exists and the engine is run by relative path (.lume/engine/bin/lume). There is no package, no installer, no .claude-plugin/, no root README. We have been polishing the post-install experience before install exists. This workstream is the correction: ship the install path.

WHERE WE ARE HEADING (the dependency chain this unlocks). The author's stated direction: (1) lume packaged as a Claude plugin, usable on other projects and gathering feedback; (2) still creator-driven for now; (3) an ultimate, long-off goal of EARNED autonomy - lume eventually making low-stakes changes itself, gated by measured value and a reputation/trust substrate, never by assertion. Two adversarial review swarms (this session, vs the more-mature theshadow27/mcp-cli, and on lume's own direction) converged on one point: goals (1) and (3) are the SAME dependency chain, not separate phases. External use (n>1) is the FIRST precondition for ever earning autonomy - you cannot measure value, build reputation, or falsify 'lume saves time on real work' from a sample of one where the framework grades itself. So this packaging workstream is not a detour from the autonomy north star; it is its first gate. The strict precondition order the reviews established: external use exists -> falsifiable value/cost measurement -> the flat 'awaiting-my-review' queue is built and used -> reversibility is mechanical and tested -> per-action-class decaying reputation -> only then reduced friction on the lowest-stakes class. Measurement and the human gate are the substrate; autonomy is the payload; never ship the payload first. Note also: the reviews flagged that 'lume making changes itself' contradicts lume's defining line ('the machine does not act without the operator') unless it means 'operator approves FASTER, the gate never moves' - that tension is for a later workstream to resolve, not this one.

WHAT THIS WORKSTREAM IS (and is not). It is the mechanical packaging and distribution work: lift the engine out of .lume/engine into an installable shape, give it a real entry point, wrap its existing verbs as Claude plugin commands, and add the front-door docs a stranger needs. The verb catalog + --json core makes this a WRAPPER, not a rewrite - commands map straight onto existing verbs. It is NOT: expanding the engine, adding the review queue or nesting, building measurement, or touching autonomy - those are later workstreams in the chain above.

NON-NEGOTIABLES carried in. Stdlib-only (the installer and packaging add no runtime deps); deterministic (no LLM in any verb/gate); behaviour-preserving (existing .lume/ workstreams and all 227 tests stay green); reuse before adding (wrap existing verbs, do not duplicate logic into plugin glue); ceremony buys its cost (no auto-upgrade, no daemon, no background process - the install path must itself be simple). Explicitly DO NOT copy mcp-cli's autonomy axis (self-driving pipeline, event bus, persistent daemon); the install path is a one-time, operator-run action.

DONE-WHEN. lume can be installed onto a fresh repo that is not this one and run there as a Claude plugin without copying .lume/engine by hand: there is a real package/entry point (so 'lume' resolves without a relative path), a .claude-plugin manifest exposing the verbs as commands, and a root README that orients a new operator (what lume is, install, the iteration loop, first command). 'lume seed' works as the new operator's first step on their repo. The existing 227 tests still pass and the live .lume/ workstreams are unaffected. A stranger could follow the README and get to their first iteration.

DISCOVERY/PLANNING DECISIONS (left open, to be resolved in the discovery + planning iterations). Exact packaging mechanism (pyproject.toml + console_script entry vs a stdlib-only zipapp/shim, given the stdlib-only constraint and that Claude plugins may have their own expected layout); the precise .claude-plugin/plugin.json shape and whether commands are thin shells over the CLI or a richer wrapper; whether the engine physically moves out of .lume/engine or is exposed in place; how an installed lume coexists with lume-developing-lume in this same repo; what the one-line install actually is and how it avoids auto-upgrade; and how much of the README is hand-written vs derived from the verb catalog. Begin with a discovery iteration that maps how Claude plugins are actually structured and audits the current layout against that, then a planning iteration that resolves these forks and slices the execution.
title: Package lume as an installable Claude plugin
